frozen_components: BERT retriever (for embedding queries and database keys)
architecture.summary: RETRO is a retrieval-enhanced autoregressive language model that splits input sequences into chunks and retrieves similar document chunks from a 2-trillion-token database using frozen BERT embeddings. Retrieved neighbours are encoded by a bi-directional transformer encoder, then integrated into the main causal decoder via a chunked cross-attention (CCA) mechanism that attends across both neighbour and time dimensions. The frozen retriever and differentiable encoder-decoder allow the model to condition token prediction on retrieved context while maintaining autoregressivity and enabling training at scale.
trained_components: decoder transformer with chunked cross-attention layers

biblio.title: Improving language models by retrieving from trillions of tokens

claims.statement: RETRO obtains comparable performance to GPT-3 and Jurassic-1 on the Pile, despite using 25× fewer parameters
The performance gain of retrieval models remains constant with model scale and is comparable to multiplying the parametric model size by 25×
Performance gain increases with retrieval database size and plateaus beyond 40 neighbours on C4 validation set
On Wikitext103, RETRO retrofitted from baseline transformer achieves perplexity of 18.97 (test) when retrieving from Wikipedia (4B tokens), compared to kNN-LM perplexity of 16.12
RETRO outperforms baseline transformer on majority of Pile test subsets; on dm_mathematics and ubuntu_irc, retrieved neighbours are not helpful and model underperforms baseline

RETRO can be retrofitted into pre-trained transformers by freezing baseline weights and training only chunked cross-attention and retrieval encoder parameters (less than 10% of weights for 7B model) on 3% of pre-training tokens
Retrieval encoder is conditioned on decoder activations through cross-attention, allowing representations to be modulated by the retrieving chunk in a differentiable way

RETRO models may benefit more easily from evaluation dataset leakage due to direct access to training dataset during evaluation; performance improvements on Wikitext103 are partly due to partial overlap with training data not caught by deduplication
